text: Standard Hastelloy N was removed from the core after exposure to a thermal fluence of $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² over a time period of 15,289 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and from outside the reactor vessel after exposure to a thermal fluence of $2.6 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm² over a time period of 20,789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The former samples were exposed to the fuel salt and the later samples were exposed to nitrogen plus 2 to $5\%$ O₂.
Postirradiation tests showed that the fracture strain was reduced at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The reduction in ductility at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is likely due to carbide precipitation and the reduction above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is due to the presence of helium from the $^{10}\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{N},\alpha)^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ transmutation.
The accumulated results in this series of experiments allow us to follow the changes in fracture strain over the thermal fluence range $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ to $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².
Two heats of modified Hastelloy N were removed from the core after irradiation to a thermal fluence of $5.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² over a time period of 9789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) is a single region reactor that is fueled by a molten fluoride salt (65 LiF-29.1 BeF $_2$ -5 ZrF $_4$ -0.9 UF $_4$ , mole %), moderated by unclad graphite, and contained by Hastelloy N (Ni-16 Mo-7 Cr-4 Fe-0.05 C, wt %).
The details of the reactor design and construction can be found elsewhere.<sup>1</sup> We knew that the neutron
The reactor went critical on June 1, 1965, and after numerous small problems were solved, assumed normal operation in May 1966.
We have removed three groups of surveillance samples and the results of tests on the Hastelloy N specimens from the first and second groups $^{2,3}$ were reported previously.

The respective history of each lot was (1) standard Hastelloy N, exposed in the MSRE cell to an environment of $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\% \mathrm{O}_2$ for 20,789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $2.6 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ , (2) standard Hastelloy N, exposed in the MSRE core to fluoride salt for 15,289 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ , and (3) modified Hastelloy N, exposed in the MSRE core to fluoride salt for 9789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $5.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
Cook and others, and the details have been reported previously. $^4$ The specimens are arranged in three stringers.
Each stringer is about 62 in. long and consists of two Hastelloy N rods and a graphite section made up of various pieces that are joined by pinning and tongue-and-groove joints.
The Hastelloy N rod has periodic-reduced sections 1 l/8 in. long by 1/8 in. in diameter and can be cut into small tensile specimens after it is removed from the reactor.
The assembled stringers fit into a perforated Hastelloy N basket that is inserted into an axial position about 3.6 in. from the core center line.
There is another surveillance facility for Hastelloy N located outside the core in a vertical position about 4.5 in. from the vessel.
These specimens are exposed to the cell environment of $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\%$ $\mathbf{O}_2$ .
These materials were given a mill anneal of 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a final anneal of 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at ORNL after fabrication.
The chemical compositions of the two modified alloys are given in Table 1.

The modifications in composition were made principally to improve the alloy's resistance to radiation damage and to bring about general improvements in the fabricability, weldability, and ductility.[5] These alloys were small 100-lb heats made by Stellite Division of Union Carbide Corporation by vacuum melting.
They were finished to $1/2$ in. plate by working at $870^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
We cut strips $1/2$ in. by $1/2$ in. from the plates and swaged them to $1/4$ -in. diam. rod.
welded together to make 62-in.-long rods for fabricating the samples.
The rods were annealed for $1\mathrm{hr}$ at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in argon and then the reduced sections were machined.
The surveillance rods inside the core are 62 in. long and those outside the vessel are 84 in. long.
They both are $\frac{1}{4}$ in. in diameter with reduced sections $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{18}$ in. long.
After removal from the reactor, the rods are sawed into small mechanical property specimens having a gage section $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{18}$ in. long.
The temperature accuracy was $\pm 0.75\%$ , the guaranteed accuracy of the Chromel-P-Alumel thermocouples used.
Revised for full-power operation at 8 Mw.

The extensometer (mechanical and electrical portions) produced measurements that could be read to about $\pm 0.02\%$ strain; however, other factors (temperature changes in the cell, mechanical vibrations, etc.) probably combined to give an overall accuracy of $\pm 0.1\%$ strain.
This error and the thermocouple accuracy combine to give a temperature uncertainty of about $\pm 1\%$ .
1 the assembly was in excellent mechanical condition when removed.
The cell environment of $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\%$ $\mathbf{O}_2$ is oxidizing to the alloy, but there is no evidence of nitriding.
There is some internal oxidation to a depth of 1 to 2 mils and a very thin uniform surface oxide.

The general microstructure is characterized by large $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ -type carbides that are distributed during the primary working and by finer $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ -type carbides that formed during the long thermal anneal of 20,789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ .

Typical photomicrographs of the standard Hastelloy N samples exposed to the MSRE fuel salt for 15,289 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are shown in Figs.
There is a thin layer of modified structure less than 0.5 mil thick at the surface.
This modified structure was noted previously.[6] As shown in Figs.
6 and 7, a similar product is formed on the surfaces of the control specimens.

From the practical standpoint, the modified layer has not changed detectably since we first observed the samples removed after 4800 hr of exposure and we feel that it will not influence the mechanical properties of the material.
The amount of fine $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ -type precipitates is not detectably different for the material aged while being irradiated (Figs.
4 and 5) and that aged in the absence of irradiation (Figs.
There does seem to be more precipitate present in heat 5065 than in heat 5085, an observation in keeping with the higher carbon content of heat 5065 (Table 1, p.
Extracted precipitates were found to be of the $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ type with a lattice parameter of 11.02 A.
Some of the material exposed to the highest thermal fluence of $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ was examined in transmission.
8 where helium bubbles are clearly visible along several of the grain boundaries.

Deformation takes place on an atomistic scale (few angstroms) and our ability to see details in the grain boundaries of such specimens is limited to about 50 A, so we are not yet able to see details of the size involved in deformation.

The thermal fluence was $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ , enough to transmute most of the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ in this material to helium.
The grain size in this material is rather large due to the high preirradiation anneal of 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the absence of the carbide stringers.

Since these alloys contain only trace quantities of iron compared with 4 to $5\%$ for the rest of the material in the MSRE and in the control facility, it is quite reasonable that iron should be deposited on the surfaces of the modified alloys.

The postirradiation tensile properties of heat 5085 after exposure to the cell environment of $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\%$ $\mathbf{O}_2$ for 20,789 hr are given in Table A-1 (Appendix) and the fracture strain is plotted as a function of test temperature in Fig.
There are significant reductions in the fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
One particularly interesting observation was that the fracture strain at room temperature could be improved by a postirradiation anneal of 8 hr at $870^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Thus, the recovery of the room-temperature ductility by this anneal supports the supposition that the reduction in ductility at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is due to carbide precipitation.

The results of tensile tests on heat 5065 after exposure to the MSRE cell environment for 20,789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are given in Table A-2 (Appendix) and the fracture strain is shown as a function of test temperature in Fig.
This heat does not exhibit the reduction in ductility at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but does show a substantial loss in ductility above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ due to irradiation.
11 for heat 5085, but the scatter in experimental results does not allow a quantitative comparison.

There is a slight reduction in the fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ due to irradiation, but the reduction is not nearly as great as noted for heat 5085 (Fig.
The fracture strain of heat 5065 is reduced above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by irradiation, and the resulting strains are lower than those for heat 5085 (Fig.
For example, at a test temperature of $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a strain rate of $0.002\,\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ heat 5085 (Fig.
13) fractures at a strain of $5\%$ and heat 5065 (Fig.
15) fractures at $3.5\%$ .
The unirradiated samples from the control facility show a greater reduction in fracture strain for heat 5085 (Fig.
14) than for heat 5065 (Fig.
16) at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but the fracture strains at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are not appreciably different for the two heats.
Heat 5085 has been carried throughout our surveillance program, so we now have accumulated enough data to see how the fracture strain is varying with neutron fluence.

At low fluence the ductility reduction at low test temperatures is restricted to $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and recovers at higher test temperatures, but at higher fluences the fracture strain is reduced over a wider temperature range.
11, the ductility can be recovered by postirradiation annealing.
The ductility reduction above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is associated with the presence of helium and the increased tendency for intergranular fracture.
The fracture strain at a given temperature above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ generally decreases with increasing thermal fluence.

17 can be attributed to thermal aging?" Several of the tensile results obtained on samples from the control facility are shown in Fig.
The results show generally that the fracture strain is reduced at all test temperatures by aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

For example, the fracture strains at test temperatures of 25 and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are 53.0 and $33.6\%$ for as-annealed samples, 38.6 and $19.6\%$ after aging for 15,289 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Fig.
19), and 28.5 and $11.0\%$ after irradiation to a fluence of $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² over a period of 15,289 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Some of the samples were tested under creep conditions at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The detailed results of these experiments are presented in Tables A-7 through A-9 (Appendix) and some correlations of these results with those obtained from previous surveillance samples<sup>7,3</sup> will be presented.
A stress-rupture plot for heat 5085 is shown in Fig.

The results obtained on the unirradiated samples show that the rupture life is reduced slightly by prolonged aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the magnitude of the effect decreasing with decreasing stress level.

The results on unirradiated samples show that the creep rate is increased slightly at high stress levels by prolonged aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A few irradiated samples tested at high stress levels and the samples irradiated to $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² have a higher minimum creep rate.
23 for the irradiated samples of heat 5085.

The scatterband shown in this figure was determined from numerous tests on samples of these same heats of standard Hastelloy N that had been irradiated in the Oak Ridge Research Reactor (ORR) to thermal fluences of 2 to $5 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².
In samples removed from the MSRE the fracture strain was decreased from about $20\%$ for unirradiated material to about $2.3\%$ by a thermal fluence of $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm².
This may be associated with the much larger times involved in the MSRE irradiations (5000 to 20,000 hr) compared with those in the ORR (1000 hr).
The effects of aging in the absence of irradiation on the fracture strain of heat 5085 at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig.
After annealing for 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the fracture strain is 30 to $40\%$ , almost independent of strain rate.
After aging for 4800 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the fracture strains are now in the range of 20 to $30\%$ .
Aging for 15,289 hr causes a small further reduction in the fracture strain at high strain rates, but no significant change in the fracture strain at low strain rates.

Two heats of modified Hastelloy N (Table 1, p.
4) were included in both the core surveillance facility and the control facility.
Heat 67-502 contained a nominal $0.5\%$ Ti and $2\%$ W and heat 67-504 contained a nominal addition of $0.5\%$ Hf.
These samples were exposed to salt 9789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and those in the MSRE received a thermal neutron fluence of $5.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².
The detailed results of tensile tests on these materials are given in Tables A-10 through A-13 (Appendix).
The fracture strains at a strain rate of $0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ are compared in Fig.
29 for the modified alloys.
In the unirradiated condition, heat 67-502 had higher fracture strains than heat 67-504 up to about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ where the opposite trend was noted.
22 and 23, for the standard alloy.
The fracture strain of the standard alloy decreases rapidly with increasing temperature above about $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a ductility minimum occurs at about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

For the modified alloys, the fracture strain does not begin to decrease with temperature until about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the strain continues to decrease up to $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the highest temperature investigated.

The fabrication sequence was (1) rods fabricated to approximate diameter, (2) rod segments welded together, (3) annealed for 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in argon, (4) sinterless ground to final diameter, (5) gage portions milled, (6) identification numbers engraved, and (7) samples put into surveillance facility.

This is likely a deposit since this alloy is very low in iron and the rest of the system is fabricated of material that contains 4 to $5\%$ Fe.

Heat 67-504 also failed by a predominately transgranular mode when tested at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Fig.
We have removed several groups of samples from the MSRE (Table 2, p.
6) and some valuable observations have been made regarding the changes of mechanical properties with fluence and thermal exposure.
The thermal flux in the center of the core is 40 times that at the vessel wall.
Only the Hastelloy N thimbles for the control rods have been exposed to the peak flux and have accumulated a thermal neutron fluence of about $1 \times 10^{21}$ neutrons/cm².
24 and 25) and the creep (Figs.

27, 29, 31, and 32) properties of Hastelloy N exposed to such a fluence are very poor; but the thimbles are stressed only slightly in compression and the observed property changes are not catastrophic.
The peak vessel thermal fluence is presently (May 1968) $2.6 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm², but the properties have already been altered markedly.
Figure 56 has been constructed for heat 5085 and shows how the fracture strain at different strain rates varies with thermal fluence at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The fraction of the strain remaining is based on the number shown in parenthesis which is the value measured in the unirradiated starting material after a 2-hr anneal at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The corresponding curve for the burnup of $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ is also

Photomicrographs of the Fracture of a Hastelloy N (Heat 67-504) Sample Exposed to Static Barren Fuel Salt for 9789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and Tested at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a Strain rate of $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ . $100\times$ (a) As polished.
shown for comparison (based on a cross section of 1930 barns for MSRE spectrum<sup>11</sup>).

The strain rate of $300\% / \mathrm{hr}$ ( $0.05 \, \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ ) is that normally used in tensile tests and the vessel heats presently, after 72,400 Mwhr of operation, will strain about $20\%$ at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ before failure.
The fracture strain decreases with decreasing strain rate to a minimum value of $1.5\%$ at a strain rate of about $0.1\% / \mathrm{hr}$ .

These property changes are quite large when we consider that only $4\%$ of the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ has been converted to helium (accumulated thermal fluence $\simeq 2.6 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ ).
However, the results from samples exposed to higher fluences in the core indicate that the property changes are not simply proportional to the quantity of $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ transmuted to helium.
Although the intended operating time for the MSRE has not been established, it likely will not exceed 362,000 Mwhr (five times the energy already generated).
Figure 56 indicates that the mechanical properties should not worsen drastically from those that prevail after 72,400 Mwhr of operation.
25 and 27, the tensile and creep properties of heat 5085 were changed just by annealing at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
31) seemed to be more resistant to aging.
The postirradiation properties of the two modified alloys (heats 67-502 and -504) were better than those of the standard alloys (compare Figs 28 and 35, pp.

The materials involved were (1) standard Hastelloy N exposed in the MSRE cell to an environment of $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\%$ $\mathbf{O}_2$ for 20,789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $2.6 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm², (2) standard Hastelloy N exposed in the MSRE core to fluoride salt for 15,289 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm², and (3) modified Hastelloy N exposed in the MSRE to fluoride salt for 9789 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $5.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².
The fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was reduced by an amount related to both the neutron fluence and the exposure time.

The fracture strain could be restored by a postirradiation anneal of 8 hr at $870^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and we propose that the reduction in fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is due to carbide precipitation.
The fracture strain in tensile tests above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and in creep tests at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was reduced by irradiation.
This embrittlement is associated with the helium that is produced by the transmutation of $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ in the alloy and cannot be recovered by postirradiation annealing.

We compared the results from samples irradiated to thermal fluences ranging from $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ to $9.4 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² and have an approximate picture of how the fracture strain varies with fluence.

This picture is complicated by the fact that the properties of the unirradiated material change some with aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and we do not have sufficient data to separate the contributions of irradiation and aging to the observed embrittlement.
Although the properties of these materials were affected appreciably by irradiation, the minimum fracture strain observed in postirradiation creep tests at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was $6\%$ .
